questions.question: Consider that 15 machines need to be connected in a LAN using 8-port Ethernet switches. Assume that these switches do not have any separate uplink ports. The minimum number of switches needed is ______
questions.to_find: Minimum number of 8-port switches required to connect 15 machines in a LAN

understand.plain: We have 15 computers to connect in a LAN using switches that each have exactly 8 ports. There are no special uplink ports, so any port used to connect two switches together is taken away from ports available for host machines. We need to find how many switches are needed so that all 15 machines can connect.

keywords.explain: A network switch with exactly 8 Ethernet ports, all of equal type — no dedicated uplink/cascade port.
keywords.example: A home switch with 8 identical RJ-45 jacks.

keywords.explain: A dedicated port on a switch used to connect to another switch or router, leaving all other ports free for hosts. Here, no such port exists.
keywords.example: Many managed switches have a separate 'uplink' or 'cascade' port for inter-switch links.

keywords.explain: A cable connecting one switch to another. Each such link consumes one port on each switch.
keywords.example: Connecting switch A port 8 to switch B port 8 uses 2 ports total across both switches.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='70' width='100' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>SW1 (8p)</text><rect x='160' y='70' width='100' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>SW2 (8p)</text><rect x='290' y='70' width='100' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>SW3 (8p)</text><line x1='130' y1='90' x2='160' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='260' y1='90' x2='290' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='145' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>link</text><text x='275' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>link</text><text x='80' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>7 hosts</text><text x='210' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>6 hosts</text><text x='340' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>6 hosts? use 7</text><text x='80' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(1 port for link)</text><text x='210' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(2 ports for links)</text><text x='340' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(1 port for link)</text><text x='210' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3 switches: 24 - 4 = 20 host ports >= 15</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three 8-port switches SW1, SW2, SW3 connected in a chain. SW1-SW2 link uses 1 port each; SW2-SW3 link uses 1 port each. Total inter-switch ports = 4, leaving 20 ports for the 15 hosts.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of 8-port switches (no dedicated uplink ports) to connect 15 machines.

terms.meaning: 15 host machines that all need to be connected to the LAN.
terms.connects: This is the demand that must be met by the available host ports.

terms.meaning: Each switch has exactly 8 ports, all of equal type.
terms.connects: Determines supply of ports; some will be consumed by inter-switch links.

terms.meaning: Connecting k switches in a spanning tree requires k-1 links, each consuming 2 ports (one on each side).
terms.example: 3 switches in a chain need 2 links = 4 ports consumed.

given.plan: Let k = number of switches. Host ports available = 8k - 2(k-1) = 6k+2. Set 6k+2 >= 15 and solve for the smallest integer k. Then verify that 2 switches are insufficient and 3 switches are sufficient.

steps.title: Derive available host ports formula
steps.apply: With k switches connected in a tree (minimum spanning), we need k-1 inter-switch links. Each link uses one port on each endpoint switch, so 2(k-1) ports are consumed. Host ports = 8k - 2(k-1) = 6k + 2.
steps.note: A chain topology (linear) is one valid minimum spanning tree; the formula holds for any tree topology.

steps.title: Check k = 2
steps.apply: With 2 switches: 6(2)+2 = 14 host ports. We need 15 ports. 14 < 15, so 2 switches are insufficient.
steps.note: This confirms we cannot do it with 2 switches.

steps.title: Check k = 3
steps.apply: With 3 switches: 6(3)+2 = 20 host ports. 20 >= 15, so 3 switches are sufficient to connect all 15 machines.
steps.note: There is plenty of margin; each switch has at least 5 free host ports in a chain layout.

solution.result: The minimum number of 8-port Ethernet switches required is 3.

questions.formula_note: Available host ports with k identical-port switches in a spanning tree: 8k - 2(k-1) = 6k+2. Minimum k satisfies 6k+2 >= number_of_hosts.

questions.question:
A host is connected to a Department network which is part of a University network. The University network, in turn, is part of the Internet. The largest network in which the Ethernet address of the host is unique is
A. the subnet to which the host belongs
B. the Department network
C. the University network
D. the Internet
questions.to_find: The largest network scope in which the Ethernet (MAC) address of a host is unique

understand.plain: An Ethernet address (also called a MAC address) is a 48-bit hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface card. MAC addresses are globally unique — no two NICs in the world share the same MAC address, because the IEEE assigns Organizationally Unique Identifiers (OUIs) to manufacturers who must ensure uniqueness within their block. Therefore the largest network where the address is unique is the entire Internet.

keywords.explain: A 48-bit hardware address assigned to a NIC at manufacturing time, written as six pairs of hex digits (e.g., 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E). Unique worldwide.
keywords.example: 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E is a globally unique MAC address.

keywords.explain: The first 24 bits of a MAC address assigned by IEEE to a manufacturer, ensuring no two manufacturers produce the same address.
keywords.example: Apple's OUI is 00:03:93; all Apple NICs start with those bits.

understand.visual_alt: Nested network hierarchy: subnet inside Department network inside University network inside the Internet. MAC address is unique at all levels including the Internet (global uniqueness).

given.aim: Determine the largest network scope in which the Ethernet MAC address is unique.

terms.meaning: 48-bit MAC address burned into the NIC hardware.
terms.connects: The property of global uniqueness determines the answer.

terms.meaning: IEEE mandates that every OUI block is unique and manufacturers must not duplicate addresses within their block.
terms.example: No two NICs worldwide share the same MAC address.
terms.connects: Global uniqueness means the address is unique even on the Internet.

terms.meaning: Subnet -> Department -> University -> Internet, each containing the previous.
terms.example: A PC in IIT Bombay CS lab: its MAC is unique on the Internet.
terms.connects: The largest scope where uniqueness holds is the Internet.

given.plan: MAC addresses are globally unique by IEEE standard. Therefore the address remains unique in the largest possible scope, which is the Internet.

steps.title: Recall MAC address uniqueness property
steps.apply: Ethernet MAC addresses are 48-bit hardware addresses assigned by manufacturers under IEEE OUI licensing. The IEEE guarantees that each OUI (first 24 bits) is unique globally, and manufacturers must ensure the remaining 24 bits are unique per OUI. This results in worldwide uniqueness.
steps.note: Unlike IP addresses, which can be reused privately, factory-assigned MAC addresses are globally unique.

steps.title: Identify the largest network scope
steps.apply: The network hierarchy is: Subnet -> Department -> University -> Internet. Since MAC address uniqueness holds globally, it holds in all these networks, including the Internet. The largest scope is therefore the Internet.
steps.note: Option D (the Internet) is correct. All smaller options (A, B, C) are subsets of the Internet, so they are weaker answers.

solution.result: D. the Internet — a MAC (Ethernet) address is globally unique, so the Internet is the largest network in which it is unique.

questions.formula_note: IEEE 802 MAC address global uniqueness: 24-bit OUI assigned to manufacturer + 24-bit device ID ensures no two NICs share the same 48-bit MAC address worldwide.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is FALSE regarding a bridge?
A. Bridge is a layer 2 device
B. Bridge reduces collision domain
C. Bridge is used to connect two or more LAN segments
D. Bridge reduces broadcast domain
questions.to_find: The FALSE statement about a bridge among the given options

understand.plain: A bridge is a Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) networking device that connects two or more LAN segments. It divides a large collision domain into smaller ones (reduces collision domains), but it still forwards broadcast frames to ALL connected segments. Therefore a bridge does NOT reduce the broadcast domain — it actually keeps all segments in the same broadcast domain. This is why option D is FALSE.

keywords.explain: A network segment where simultaneous transmissions cause collisions. Bridges separate collision domains — frames are not forwarded if the destination is on the same segment.
keywords.example: Two hosts on the same hub share a collision domain; a bridge between two hubs creates separate collision domains.

keywords.explain: A set of devices that all receive a broadcast frame. Bridges forward broadcasts to ALL ports, so all bridge-connected segments remain in one broadcast domain.
keywords.example: A router separates broadcast domains; a bridge does not.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='180' viewBox='0 0 400 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='70' width='120' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Segment A</text><rect x='160' y='70' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Bridge</text><rect x='270' y='70' width='120' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Segment B</text><line x1='130' y1='90' x2='160' y2='90' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='90' x2='270' y2='90' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='70' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Collision domain 1</text><text x='330' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Collision domain 2</text><text x='200' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>One broadcast domain (both sides)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A bridge connects Segment A and Segment B. Each segment is its own collision domain (bridge reduces collision domains). However, both segments share one broadcast domain because bridge forwards broadcasts to all ports.

given.aim: Identify the FALSE statement about a bridge.

terms.meaning: Operates at the Data Link Layer; uses MAC addresses to make forwarding decisions.
terms.example: A bridge reads the destination MAC in a frame and decides which port to forward it out.

terms.meaning: Each port on a bridge is a separate collision domain. Traffic on Segment A does not collide with traffic on Segment B.
terms.example: Before bridge: 10 hosts in one collision domain. After bridge splitting 5+5: two separate collision domains.

terms.meaning: A bridge forwards broadcast frames out of ALL ports (except the ingress port). It does not filter broadcasts.
terms.example: An ARP request broadcast from Segment A is forwarded to Segment B by the bridge.
terms.connects: Statement D (bridge reduces broadcast domain) is FALSE — bridges do not reduce broadcast domains.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: A is true (Layer 2), B is true (reduces collision domains), C is true (connects LAN segments), D is false (bridges do NOT reduce broadcast domains; that requires a router).

steps.title: Verify TRUE statements
steps.apply: A: Bridge is a Layer 2 (Data Link) device — TRUE. It uses MAC addresses. B: Bridge reduces collision domain — TRUE. Each bridge port is its own collision domain. C: Bridge connects two or more LAN segments — TRUE. That is the primary function of a bridge.
steps.note: All three (A, B, C) are well-known correct properties of bridges.

steps.title: Evaluate statement D
steps.apply: D: 'Bridge reduces broadcast domain' — FALSE. A bridge forwards broadcast frames to ALL connected segments. All bridge-connected segments remain in one large broadcast domain. Only a Layer 3 device (router/Layer 3 switch) separates broadcast domains.
steps.note: A key distinction: bridges = separate collision domains but same broadcast domain; routers = separate both.

solution.result: D. Bridge reduces broadcast domain — this is FALSE. Bridges forward broadcasts to all segments and do not reduce broadcast domains.

questions.formula_note: Bridge (Layer 2): segments collision domains but forwards broadcasts to all ports, keeping all connected segments in one broadcast domain. Routers (Layer 3) separate broadcast domains.

questions.question:
A router has two full-duplex Ethernet interfaces each operating at 100 Mb/s. Ethernet frames are at least 84 bytes long (including the Preamble and the Inter-Packet-Gap). The maximum packet processing time at the router for wire-speed forwarding to be possible is (in microseconds)
A. 0.01
B. 3.36
C. 6.72
D. 8
questions.to_find: Maximum packet processing time at the router for wire-speed forwarding (in microseconds)

understand.plain: Wire-speed forwarding means the router must finish processing and forwarding each incoming packet before the next one arrives (so it never falls behind). The minimum-size Ethernet frame takes a certain amount of time to be transmitted at 100 Mb/s. The router must complete processing within that time window. We calculate how long it takes to transmit an 84-byte frame at 100 Mb/s — that is the maximum processing budget.

keywords.explain: The router processes packets as fast as they arrive on the wire, without any queuing or back-pressure. Processing one packet must complete before the next minimum-size packet finishes arriving.
keywords.example: A wire-speed router at 100 Mb/s can handle the full 100 Mb/s input rate without dropping packets.

keywords.explain: Including preamble (8 bytes) and inter-packet gap (12 bytes), the minimum Ethernet frame occupies 84 bytes on the wire, translating to 672 bits.
keywords.example: A 64-byte payload frame + 8-byte preamble + 12-byte IPG = 84 bytes on wire.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing successive 84-byte Ethernet frames arriving at 100 Mb/s. The router must process each frame within the 6.72 microsecond window before the next frame starts arriving.

given.aim: Find the maximum processing time per packet so that the router can keep up with 100 Mb/s wire speed.

terms.meaning: 100 Mb/s = 100 x 10^6 bits per second on each interface.
terms.connects: Determines how quickly frames arrive (and must be forwarded).

terms.meaning: 84 bytes = 672 bits (including preamble and inter-packet gap).
terms.connects: This determines the minimum inter-frame interval — the tightest processing deadline.

terms.meaning: The time between the start of one minimum frame and the start of the next, which is also the transmission time of one 84-byte frame.
terms.example: If frames arrive every T seconds, processing must finish within T seconds.
terms.connects: Maximum processing time = transmission time of 84 bytes at 100 Mb/s.

given.plan: Compute the transmission time for an 84-byte (672-bit) frame at 100 Mb/s. This equals the maximum allowable processing time for wire-speed operation.

steps.title: Convert frame size to bits
steps.apply: The minimum Ethernet frame is 84 bytes on the wire. 84 * 8 = 672 bits.
steps.note: This 84 bytes includes: 7-byte preamble + 1-byte SFD + 6+6 src/dst + 2-byte type + 46-byte min payload + 4-byte FCS = 72 bytes payload + 12-byte IPG = 84 bytes total on wire.

steps.title: Compute transmission time
steps.note: This is the time between the start of one minimum frame and the start of the next. The router must process each packet within this window.

solution.result: C. 6.72 microseconds — the maximum packet processing time for wire-speed forwarding at 100 Mb/s with 84-byte minimum frames.

questions.formula_note: Transmission time = frame_size_in_bits / link_speed. Wire-speed forwarding requires processing time <= transmission time of the minimum-size frame.

questions.question:
Suppose the round trip propagation delay for a 10 Mbps Ethernet having jamming signal is 46.4 us. The minimum frame size is:
A. 94
B. 416
C. 464
D. 512
questions.to_find: Minimum frame size in bits for 10 Mbps Ethernet with given round-trip delay and jamming signal

understand.plain: In CSMA/CD (used in Ethernet), a sender must keep transmitting until it can detect any collision. A collision can happen at the far end of the cable, and the jamming signal must travel back. The minimum frame transmission time must be at least equal to the round-trip propagation delay plus the jamming signal duration. This ensures the sender can detect the collision while still transmitting. We multiply this total time by the data rate to get the minimum frame size in bits.

keywords.explain: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. A sender detects a collision during transmission and sends a jamming signal, then backs off and retransmits.
keywords.example: Two hosts on Ethernet both start transmitting at the same time — CSMA/CD detects the collision.

keywords.explain: After detecting a collision, the sender transmits a 48-bit jam signal to ensure all stations on the network detect the collision.
keywords.example: 32-bit or 48-bit pattern broadcast after collision detection in standard Ethernet.

keywords.explain: The frame must be long enough that the sender is still transmitting when the collision signal returns. Minimum frame transmission time >= RTT propagation delay + jamming time.
keywords.example: Standard 10BASE5 Ethernet: minimum frame 64 bytes = 512 bits at 10 Mbps over 2500m.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='160' viewBox='0 0 420 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='400' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='50' class='sub ink'>Sender</text><text x='360' y='50' class='sub ink'>Receiver</text><line x1='30' y1='55' x2='390' y2='105' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><line x1='390' y1='105' x2='30' y2='140' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='150' y='72' class='sub ink'>frame propagation (46.4/2 us)</text><text x='150' y='125' class='sub mut'>jam signal returns (46.4/2 us)</text><text x='100' y='160' class='sub ink'>Total = RTT(46.4) + jam(4.8) = 51.2 us -> 512 bits</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline: sender transmits frame, signal travels to far end (23.2 us), collision occurs, jam signal travels back (23.2 us) plus jam duration (4.8 us). Sender must still be transmitting at the end of this period: total 51.2 us = 512 bits at 10 Mbps.

given.aim: Calculate the minimum Ethernet frame size given RTT propagation delay and jamming signal size.

terms.example: Signal takes 23.2 us to reach far end and 23.2 us to return.
terms.connects: Contributes the dominant term to minimum frame transmission time.

terms.meaning: 48-bit signal sent after collision detection. Transmission time = 48 / (10 * 10^6) = 4.8 microseconds.
terms.connects: Added to RTT to get total minimum frame transmission time.
Used to convert time to bits: size = time * speed.

given.plan: Minimum frame transmission time = RTT + jamming time. Convert jamming bits to time. Multiply total time by link speed to get minimum frame size in bits.

steps.title: Compute jamming signal transmission time
steps.apply: Jamming signal = 48 bits. At 10 Mbps: t_jam = 48 / (10 * 10^6) = 4.8 * 10^-6 s = 4.8 us.
steps.note: Standard IEEE 802.3 specifies a 48-bit (6-byte) jam sequence.

steps.title: Compute minimum frame transmission time
steps.apply: The sender must keep transmitting for the entire round-trip so it can detect collisions. Minimum transmission time = RTT propagation delay + jamming transmission time = 46.4 + 4.8 = 51.2 microseconds.
steps.note: Some formulations use t_min = 2 * propagation delay (the RTT) + jam. Here RTT is already given as the round-trip.

steps.title: Convert time to frame size in bits
steps.apply: Minimum frame size = 51.2 us * 10 Mbps = 51.2 * 10^-6 * 10 * 10^6 = 512 bits.
steps.note: 512 bits = 64 bytes, which is the well-known minimum Ethernet frame size (excluding preamble and IPG in the standard definition).

solution.result: D. 512 bits — the minimum frame size is 512 bits (64 bytes) for 10 Mbps Ethernet with 46.4 us round-trip propagation delay and 48-bit jamming signal.

questions.formula_note: CSMA/CD minimum frame size: min_frame_bits = (RTT_propagation_delay + jam_transmission_time) * link_speed_bps. Ensures sender detects collision before frame transmission ends.

questions.question: A link has transmission speed of 10^6 bits/sec. It uses data packets of size 1000 bytes each. Assume that the acknowledgment has negligible transmission delay and that its propagation delay is the same as the data propagation delay. Also, assume that the processing delays at nodes are negligible. The efficiency of the stop-and-wait protocol in this setup is exactly 20%. The value of the one way propagation delay in milliseconds is ______
questions.to_find: One-way propagation delay in milliseconds given stop-and-wait efficiency of 20%

understand.plain: In the stop-and-wait protocol, the sender transmits one packet and waits for an ACK before sending the next. The efficiency is the fraction of time the link is actually busy sending data. It equals the packet transmission time divided by the total round-trip time (transmission time + 2 * one-way propagation delay). Given efficiency is 20%, we can solve for the one-way propagation delay.

keywords.explain: A flow control protocol where the sender transmits one frame and waits for an acknowledgment (ACK) before sending the next frame. Simple but potentially inefficient.
keywords.example: Sender sends packet, waits, receiver sends ACK, sender gets ACK, then sends next packet.

keywords.explain: The fraction of total time the link is used productively for data transmission. Efficiency = Tt / (Tt + 2*Tp) for stop-and-wait.
Time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver along the link. For stop-and-wait, the total wait includes Tp for data and Tp for ACK return.
keywords.example: A 1600 km fiber link at 2*10^8 m/s has Tp = 8 ms one-way.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='160' viewBox='0 0 400 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='60' height='120' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Sender</text><rect x='330' y='20' width='60' height='120' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Receiver</text><rect x='70' y='30' width='80' height='20' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='44' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Tt = 8ms</text><line x1='150' y1='50' x2='330' y2='80' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Tp</text><line x1='330' y1='80' x2='70' y2='110' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='190' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>ACK (Tp)</text><text x='200' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Efficiency = Tt / (Tt + 2Tp) = 8/(8+2p) = 0.2 -> p = 16ms</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Stop-and-wait timeline: sender transmits for Tt=8ms, data travels Tp, receiver sends ACK, ACK travels back Tp. Total cycle = Tt+2Tp. Efficiency = 8/(8+2p) = 0.2, giving p=16ms.

given.aim: Find the one-way propagation delay given stop-and-wait efficiency = 20%.

terms.meaning: Time to push all bits of one packet onto the link. Tt = packet_size / link_speed.
Time for a bit to travel from sender to receiver across the link. ACK takes the same time to return.
terms.connects: Appears as 2*Tp in the denominator of the efficiency formula.
Setting efficiency = 0.20 and solving gives Tp = 16 ms.

given.plan: Compute Tt = packet_size / link_speed. Use efficiency formula: Tt / (Tt + 2*Tp) = 0.20. Solve for Tp.

steps.title: Compute packet transmission time
steps.apply: Packet size = 1000 bytes = 8000 bits. Link speed = 10^6 bps. Tt = 8000 / 10^6 = 0.008 s = 8 ms.
steps.note: Both data and ACK propagation delays are equal (Tp each way).

steps.title: Apply stop-and-wait efficiency formula
steps.note: The ACK has negligible transmission delay, so the full round-trip wait is Tt (data) + Tp (data travels) + Tp (ACK returns) = Tt + 2*Tp.

solution.result: The one-way propagation delay is 16 milliseconds.

questions.formula_note: Stop-and-wait protocol efficiency: eta = Tt / (Tt + 2*Tp), where Tt = transmission time of data packet and Tp = one-way propagation delay.
